title: Beguni

ingredients.name: brinjal (medium size), cut into 5mm rounds
visual_checks: A medium to large brinjal gives the best-sized rounds for frying; avoid very thin or very wide pieces.
ingredients.notes: A medium to large brinjal gives the best-sized rounds for frying; avoid very thin or very wide pieces.

subs.name: Courgette (zucchini) rounds, raw banana slices, or sliced raw plantain work beautifully in the same batter.
A 50:50 mix of rice flour and besan gives a lighter, crispier fritter that stays crunchy for longer.

ingredients.name: turmeric powder
visual_checks: Split between the batter and the salted brinjal.
ingredients.notes: Split between the batter and the salted brinjal.

ingredients.name: cumin seeds
visual_checks: Stirred into the batter; adds a earthy note.
ingredients.notes: Stirred into the batter; adds a earthy note.

ingredients.name: kalonji (nigella seeds)
visual_checks: A distinctly Bangladeshi touch; adds a faint onion-pepper flavour to the batter.
ingredients.notes: A distinctly Bangladeshi touch; adds a faint onion-pepper flavour to the batter.

subs.name: Celery seeds or caraway seeds provide a similar aromatic peppery note if kalonji is unavailable.

ingredients.name: water
visual_checks: Adjust until batter is thick enough to coat a spoon without dripping off instantly.
ingredients.notes: Adjust until batter is thick enough to coat a spoon without dripping off instantly.

ingredients.name: cooking oil
visual_checks: For deep frying; the oil should be deep enough to submerge the fritters.
ingredients.notes: For deep frying; the oil should be deep enough to submerge the fritters.

steps.text: Sprinkle the brinjal rounds with ¼ tsp turmeric and a little salt. Toss gently to coat and leave for 5 minutes — the salt draws out moisture and helps the batter adhere better.

Mix the besan, remaining ¼ tsp turmeric, red chilli powder, cumin seeds, kalonji (if using), and a good pinch of salt in a wide bowl. Gradually whisk in the water until a smooth, thick batter forms — it should coat the back of a spoon in an even layer without running off quickly.

Pat the salted brinjal rounds dry with a cloth or paper towel. Heat the oil in a kadai over medium-high heat until a tiny drop of batter sizzles and floats to the surface immediately — the oil is ready.

Dip a brinjal round in the batter, letting excess drip off, then slide it gently into the hot oil. Fry 4–5 rounds at a time without crowding. Cook for 2–3 minutes, flipping once, until both sides are deep golden and the batter is crisp.

Lift the fritters out with a slotted spoon and drain on a plate lined with paper towels. Repeat with the remaining brinjal. Serve immediately while hot and crisp, with mustard paste or green chutney on the side.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: brinjal → Courgette (zucchini) rounds, raw banana slices, or sliced raw plantain work beautifully in the same batter.; besan → A 50:50 mix of rice flour and besan gives a lighter, crispier fritter that stays crunchy for longer.; kalonji → Celery seeds or caraway seeds provide a similar aromatic peppery note if kalonji is unavailable.
indian_kitchen_adaptation: This is essentially the same as South Indian vazhakai bajji or North Indian baingan pakoda — the technique is identical. Besan is a pantry staple across India. Kalonji is sold as 'kalonji' or 'mangrail' in Indian spice shops and is worth adding for an authentic Bangladeshi character. Fry in any deep kadai; a splatter guard is helpful.
